text: ALTENBURG, a village of Lower Austria, 25 m. N of Krems, on the l. bank of the Kamp. Pop. 249. A celebrated abbey of Benedictines was founded here in 1144.—Also a town of Hungary, the cap. of the co. of Weisselburg, on the Leitha, 20 m. SSE of Presburg. Pop. 3,500. It conducts an active commerce in grain and cattle.—Also a town of Transylvania, on the White Koros river, 45 m. W of Karlsburg.—Also a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the circle of Alsfeld, on the Schwalm. Alt. above sea-level 1,179 ft. Pop. 446.

ALTENDORF, a village of Bavaria, 10 m. SSE of Bamberg, on the Regnitz. Pop. 234. The French under Kleber defeated the Austrians in the neighbourhood of this v. on the 9th of August, 1796. On the 15th of June, 1816, it suffered greatly from an inundation of the river.—Also a v. of Brunswick, in the circle of Holzminden.—Also a v. of Moravia, in the circle of Olmutz, 15 m. NNE of Neustadt.—Also a v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Schweitz, on the lake of Zurich.-Also a v. of Saxe-Altenburg, on the Kahla. Pop. 193.
ALTENFELD, a town of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, on the Oelze river. Pop. 800.

ALTENFIORD, a gulf on the N coast of Finmark, under the 70th parallel, at the mouth of the Alten river. Its shores are thickly wooded. Some barley is grown on sheltered sites, which form the limit of cultivation in the N of Europe. The bottom of the bay skirts the base of mountains exceeding 1,000 ft. in height. ALT
ALTENHEIM, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, 6 m. W of Offenberg. Pop. 1,500.
ALTENHOFEN, a town of Austria, in Illyria, on the Metzruth, 4 m. N of St. Veit.
ALTENHUNTORF, a village of Oldenberg, 9 m. ENE of Elsfleth. Count Gerard defeated the Bremens in the neighbourhood of this v. in 1475.

ALTENKIRCHEN, a town and circle of Prussia, in the district of Coblentz, on the Wied. Pop. of town 1,200; of circle in 1831, 31,274, of whom 16,244 were Protestants, and 14,843 Roman Catholics. Near this town, on the 1st of June, 1796, the French under Kleber defeated the Austrians under the prince of Wurtemberg; and on the 9th of September following, the French were defeated, and their commander Marçeau killed, by the Austrians under Hotze.—Also a small town in the Prussian prov. of Clevesberg, 8 m. SSE of Gueldres.—Also a town of Bavaria, in the Isar circle, 6 m. N of Freiswig.

ALTENMARKT, a village of Austria, in Styria, on the r. bank of the Ens river, 34 m. NW of Brug. Pop. 300.—Also a v. of Austria, on the Triestingthal, at the foot of the Hochecks. Pop. 260, chiefly employed in making cutlery.—Also a town of Bavaria, in the Isar circle, at the confluence of the Alz and the Traun. Pop. 300.
ALTENSALZE, a village of Prussia, in Saxony, 5 m NNW of Calbe, celebrated for its salt-springs. Pop. 500.

ALTENSTADT, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, on the Nidder, 12 m NE of Offenbach. Pop. 901.—Also a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, on the Iller. Pop. 630.—Also a v. of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, on the Zusammen. Pop. 786.—Also a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, on the r. bank of the Lauter, 2 m. SE of Wissembourg. Pop. 1,442.

ALTENSTEIG, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black Forest, 5 m. W by N of Nagold, on the Nagold river, at an alt. of 1,500 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 1,950.—Also a v. of Lower Austria, 55 m. NW of Vienna, on the road to Budweiss.

ALTENSTEIN, a hamlet of Saxe-Meiningen, 10 m. S of Eisenach, in the neighbourhood of which is the ducal castle, and several spots ennobled by their historical association with the great German reformer. -Also a v. of Bavaria, in the circle of the Lower Maine, at the confluence of a small stream with the Bausach, 23 m. NNW of Bamberg. Pop. 400.
ALTENWAHLEN, a village of Hanover, 12 m. SE of Verden, near the r. bank of the Aller.
ALTENWERDER, a village of Hanover, in Luneburg, on an island of the same name in the Elbe, between Hamburg and Harburg. Pop. 1,055.
ALTERADO, a river of Potosi, in Upper Bolivia, flowing in an ESE course to the Rio San Juan, which it joins on the l. bank, 40 m. above Suipacha.

ALTER-DO-CHAO, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, 20 m. S by W of Santarem, on the Amazon, at the embouchure of the Tapajoz.—Also a town of Portugal, in Alemtejo, 12 m. W by S of Portalegre, on the Avis river. Pop. 511.

ALTERSWEILEN, or ALTENSWYLEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, 3 m. SSW of Constanz, historically celebrated for the victory which the Swiss obtained in its neighbourhood, in April 1499, over the forces of the emperor Maximilian I.
ALTFAEHR, a town of Prussia, in Pomerania, on the W coast of Rugen.
ALTGEBIRG, a village of Hungary, 9 m. N of Newsohl. Pop. 800. Copper is mined in the vicinity.
ALTHART, a town of Moravia, on the Taja, 4 m. SW of Jamnitz.

ALTHEIM, a town of Austria, in the circle of the Inn, 6 m. E of Brunau.—Also a town of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine. Pop. 1,080.—Also a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, 7 m. N of Albech. Pop. 1,080. Count Eberhard defeated the forces of the Imperial towns in the neighbourhood of this town in 1322.—Also, a v. of Baden, in the circle of the Lake, NE of Ueberlingen. Pop. 274.
ALTHENGSTATT, a village of Wurtemberg, in the bailiwick of Merklingen. Pop. 600.
ALTHOF, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Konigsberg. Pop. 314.
ALTHOFEN, a town of Austria, in Carinthia, 18 m. NE of Klagenfurt. Pop. 800.
ALTHORNE, a parish in Essex, 4 m. NW of Burnham. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 418.

ALTHORPE, a parish in Lincoln, in Manley wapentake. Area 5,460 acres. Pop. 1,184. The township of A. is 4 m. SE from Crowle. Pop. 366.—Also a manor in the p. of Great Brington, Northamptonshire, from which Earl Spencer derives the title of viscount; and the manor-house of which has European celebrity for the magnificent library it contains.
ALTHORPE'S ISLES, a group of three islets in Spencer's gulf on the S coast of Australia. The largest is about a mile in extent.
ALTHUTTEN, a town of Bohemia, in the lordship of Dobrisch. Pop. 524.
ALTIER, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. and 18 m. E by S of Mende. Pop. 1,292. Zurich, on the Thur. Pop. 1,209.
ALTILIA, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria Ultra, on the Nielo, 4 m. N by W of San- Severina.
ALTILLAO, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Corrèze, cant. of Mercœur. Pop. 1,805.

ALTIN, a lake of Siberia, in the gov. of Tomsk, in N lat. 52°, E long. 87°. It is of irregular outline, stretching at certain seasons 80 m. in length from N to S, and in some places attaining a width of 50 m. It appears to be formed by the waters descending from the northern side of the Little Altai chain, and from the SW extremity of the Kusnetz chain; and will of course vary greatly in its dimensions according to the season of the year and the quantity of melted snow which it may receive from these chains, both of which soar above the snow line. At its S extremity Mount Tulale attains an elevation of 6,000 ft. above sea-level, or 4,100 ft. above the surface of the lake; but considerably higher summits must contribute their waters to the basin of this lake. At its northern extremity, a narrow arm runs off westwards, from which a river called the Biya issues, and flowing successively in a W, N, and W course, with a very rapid current, joins the Katunga below Büsk, where the united streams take the name of the Obi or Ob. The Biya appears to enter by several arms near 51° 40' N. It also bears the name of the Kulishman. The Altin lake is called by the Russians Teletskoi Oseroe from the Teletsi, a Tartar tribe in the vicinity, who call it ALTIN KUL; while the Kalmuks call it ALTAI NUR OF ALTIN NUR.
ALTINGEN, a village of Wurtemberg, 6 m. SSE of Herrenberg. Pop. 915.

ALTINO, a village of Lombardy, 11 m. N of Venice, on the Sile, supposed to mark the site of the ancient Altinum from whose ruins a considerable part of Venice has been built.—Also a town of Naples, 9 m. S of Lanciano.
ALTISRIED, a town of Bavaria, in the Upper Danube circle, on the Iller. Pop. 580.

ALTISSIMO MONTE, one of the loftiest summits in the Apennine chain, in Tuscany, near Serravezza. Michael Angelo obtained much of his purest ALT has been extensively quarried upon it, at an alt. of from 2,700 to 3,500 ft. above sea-level; while its lower regions yield many beautiful varieties of variegated marble.

ALTKIRCH, an arrondissement, cant., com., and town of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin. The arrond. has an area of 114,170 hectares, with a pop. in 1846 of 148,274. It comprises the 7 cant. of Altkirch, Ferrette, Habsheim, Hirsingue, Huningue, Landser, and Mulhausen.—The cant. comprises 28 com.—The com. had a pop. of 3,028 in 1836.—The town, which is also the chief place in the arrond., is seated on an eminence near the Ill river, on the high road from Paris to Basle, 31 m. S of Colmar. It has some trade in gypsum which is quarried in the neighbourhood, and in lint, cattle, and wheat.
ALTKONIG, one of the loftiest summits of the Hoheberg in Nassau, 15 m. NW of Frankfort-on-the- Maine. Alt. 2,468 ft.
ALTLAND, a district of Transylvania, comprising the counties of Herrmannstadt, Leschkirk, Gross- Shenk, and Reps.

ALTMUHL, a river of Bavaria, rising near the Windelsbach, 9 m. E of Rottenburg, and flowing into the Danube, on the l. bank, at Kelheim, after a SE course of 150 m., in which it passes Leutershausen, Herrieden, Gunzenhausen, Eichstadt, Beilngries, and Dietfurt. It has been proposed to unite the Black sea with the German ocean by a canal passing through

ALTIKON, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of the centre of Europe,—a project first conceived by Charlemagne, - from Dietfurt on the Altmuhl to Bamberg on the Regnitz. The estimated length of this canal is 592,543 Bavarian, or 563,900 English ft., or 107 m. Its proposed dimensions are 54 ft. at top, and 34 ft. at bottom; with a depth of 5 ft. The estimated cost is £820,000. The summit-level is at Neumarkt on the Sulz, which is 300 ft. above the level of the Danube at Kelheim, and 360 ft. above that of the Regnitz at Bamberg. The number of locks will be 94.
ALTMUNSTER, or ALTMUHL MUNSTER, a town of Bavaria, on the Altmuhl, 17 m. NNE of Ingolstadt. Pop. 780.
ALTNAU. See ALTENAU.
ALTO, or AITO, a town of Turkey, in the pashalik of Janina, on the r. bank of the Fidari.
ALTO CHINERAE, a town of Chili, in the prov. of Coquimbo, near the source of the Chinerae river in the Andes.
ALTOFANNI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 14 m. SE of Montpelosa.
ALTO INEZ, a river of Chili, in the prov. of Coquimbo, flowing into the Juncal.
ALTO DI JACAIBAMBA, a pass in the Bolivian Andes, on the road from Lima to Pasco. Alt. 15,135 ft.
ALTO DE LACHAGUAL, another pass in the Bolivian Andes. Alt. 15,480 ft.
ALTOMONTE, a town of Naples, in Lower Calabria, 12 m. SW of Cassano. Pop. 4,600. Saltmines are wrought in the vicinity.

ALTON, a parish and town of Hants, on the river Wey, 47 m. W by S of London. Area of p. 3,910 acres. Pop. 3,139.—The town is agreeably situated near the Wey, 10 m. NE of New Alresford, on the post-road from Winchester to London. The Farnham branch of the South-western railway is now extending to A. It is one of the polling-places for the county. The town gives name to one of the fourteen divisions of the county, having an area of 68,730 acres, with a pop. in 1841 of 15,308.

ALTON, a town in Madison co., in the state of Illinois, U. S., on the E bank of the Mississippi, 23 m. above the mouth of the Missouri, 82 m. W by S marble from this mountain; and of late years marble | from Springfield. It is finely situated for the commerce of the river, and has an excellent natural wharf. Bituminous coal exists in great abundance near the town. Pop. in 1840, 2,340.—Also a township of New Hampshire, U. S., 25 m. NW of Dover, on Lake Winnepisiogee. Pop. 2,000.

A'LTONA, a city of Denmark, in the lordship of Pinneberg, an appanage of the duchy of Holstein, in N lat. 53° 32' 45", E long. 9° 56' [Conn. des Temps]; on the right side of the Elbe, 75 m. from the mouth of the river, and 2 m. below Hamburg. The territories of the two cities are divided from each other by a wall running through a large open field or common; but the warehouses and wharfs stretch in one unbroken line along the river. This city-next to Copenhagen the most important in the kingdom—is divided into four parts, which are distinguished by the names of the cardinal points: and consists of about 85 streets, 6 squares, and 3,120 houses, generally well-built, with high pointed roofs. The principal public buildings are a Catholic and 5 Protestant churches, 2 synagogues, 3 hospitals, a mint, a gymnasium founded in 1730 by Christian VI., a public library, a patriotic institution for the duchies of Holstein and Sleswig, a theatre, a royal lottery, two medical and several German schools, a commercial academy, a bank, and an exchange. The advantageous situation of A. renders it the entrepot of considerable commerce both inland and maritime. The port is safe and commodious. The most common destinations of its vessels, which amount to about 100 in number, are, exclusive of the ports in the Baltic and North seas, the Mediterranean, and the various fisheries for herring, cod, whales, and seals. Ship-building constitutes one of the most important branches of employment in the city; but it possesses also manufactures of silk, velvet, cotton and linen fabrics, wax-cloth, paper-hangings, sail-cloth, parchment, and tobacco, besides tanneries, printing establishments, type-foundries, anchor-forges, sugarrefineries, breweries, distilleries of brandy, &c. A railway has been executed from A. to Kiel, a distance of 84 m., with a branch of 14 m. from Kiel to Gluckstadt via Elmshorn, and another branch of 25 m. from Newmunster to Rendsburg, which it is proposed to push forward to Neustadt. The number of passengers carried on these three lines in 1846 was 594,812, while the merchandise, exclusive of cattle, amounted to 93,370 tons of 1,000 kilogrammes each.—A. which, previous to the union of the lordship of Pinneberg in 1640 to the crown of Denmark, was a small village, rose rapidly in importance after that period, and in 1664 received the privileges of a city. Early in the following century it was almost entirely destroyed by the Swedish general Steinbock. The pop. in 1778 was 24,400; in 1831, 26,500; in 1844, 30,000.
ALTON BARNES, a parish in Wiltshire, 4½ m. W by N from Pewsey. Area 250 acres. Pop. 167.
ALTON PANCRAS, a parish in Dorsetshire, 2 m. E from Cerne Abbas. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 1,342.
ALTOPASCIO, a district and v. of Tuscany, 3 m. S of Montecarlo. Pop. 1,100. The railway from Pisa to Pistoja was opened to A., a distance of 21 m., in 1848.
ALTORF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Molsheim. Pop. 937.
ALTOS, a river of Peru, in the prov. of Truxillo, one of the head-branches of the Huallaga.
ALTOS DE LOS HUESSOS, a pass in the Bolivian Andes, in S lat. 16° 21', at the foot of the volcano of Arequipa. Alt. 13,573 ft.
ALTOS DE TOLEDO, a pass in the Bolivian Andes, in S lat. 16° 2'. Alt. 15,528 ft.
ALTOS DE TRONCOSO, a town of La Plata, on the r. bank of the Saladille, 110 m. S of Buenos Ayres.
ALTRACAZ, or ALCATRACES (POINT), a headland on the W side of the gulf of Paria; in N lat. 10° 25', W long. 62° 28'. ALT

ALTRANSTADT, a town of Prussia, in Saxony, 10 m. E of Merseburg. Pop. 370. There is an ancient castle here, in which Charles dictated the treaty of 24th Sept., 1706, by which Augustus II. renounced the crown of Poland. It is the Antiqua Ranstede of the Middle ages.
ALTREN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Soleure, on the Aar, 1,450 ft. above sea-level.

ALTRINCHAM, or ALTRINGHAM, a chapelry and market-town in Bowdon parish, Cheshire, 8 m. SW from Manchester, with which it is connected by railway. Area of chapelry 520 acres. Pop. 3,990. The principal manufacture of the town consists in yarnspinning and cotton-weaving.
ALTRIPP, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Rhine. Pop. 460.
ALTSATTEL, a village of Bohemia, in the circle and 3 m. SW of Elnbogen.
ALTSCHWEYER, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Kinzig, 3 m. SSW of Baden. Pop. 816.
ALTSOHL, a mining-town of Hungary, in the co. of Zolyom, at the confluence of the Gran and the Salatina, 9 m. S of Neusohl. Pop. 1,800. There are mineral springs in the neighbourhood.

ALTSTADT, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Tabor, 7 m. SE of New Frisnitz. Pop. 1,100.— Also a town of Moravia, circle and 40 m. N of Olmutz, at the foot of the Schneeberg. Pop. 1,800.— Also a town of Saxony, in the Erzgeberg circle, 4 m. NNE of Glauchau. Pop. 1,100.—Also an islet in the lake of Waldstetten, 3 m. SE of Lucerne in Switzerland.—See also Allstadt.

ALTSTETTEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Saint Gall, on the Rheinthal, 9 m. NE of Appenzell. Pop. 1,800, chiefly muslin-weavers. Alt. above sea-level 1,600 ft. There are numerous springs in the vicinity.
ALTUMUR, a village of Afghanistan, on the Sufeid Koh, in N lat. 33° 57'.
ALTUN. See ALTIN.

ALTU'N KU'PRI, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Kurdistan, on an island in the Altun Su or Little Zab, 40 m. SSE of Erbil. Pop. 2,000. Col. Shiel found the river, in the month of August, about 50 yards wide above the island. The name signifies 'Golden bridge,' and is supposed, says Col. Shiel, to have been given this place on account of the lucrative toll formerly levied here; but a simpler derivation is from the river itself.

ALTUN SU, a river of Kurdistan, rising in the district of Ushneh, in Persia, near Lake Urumiyah, and flowing into the Tigris 12 m. W of Altun Kupri. The Alauta or Aluta of Wallachia, is called Altun Su or 'Golden river' by the Turks, "from the golden particles," says Kinneir, "which are sometimes found in its bed."

ALTUN TASH, a town of Asiatic Turkey, 24 m. S of Kutaia, in N lat. 39° 4', on the l. bank of the Pursek. The name, signifying 'Golden stone,' has perhaps been suggested by the yellow coloured rocks in the vicinity.
ALTURA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, 2 m. W of Segorbe. Pop. 1,500. It has some trade in wine and brandy, which are manufactured in the town and environs.
ALTURAS, a town of Portugal, in Tras-os-Montes, on the Beca river, 9 m. SW by W of Chaves.
ALTWARP, a town of Prussia, in Pomerania, 8 m. E of Ukermonde, on the coast of the Little Huff. Pop. 850.

ALTWASSER, a town of Austria, in Moravia, 2 m. NNW of Liebau. Pop. 350. Its local name is STARA WODA.—Also a village of Prussia, in Silesia, on the Polsnitz, 2 m. N of Waldenburg. Pop. 1,550. Coal and iron are wrought in the vicinity.
ALTZENAU, or ALTZEY, a canton and town of Hesse-Darmstadt. The town is on the Selze, at an alt. of 793 ft. above sea-level, 5 m. SE of Hanau. Pop. 500.
ALUCCIA (CAPE), a point of land on the E coast of Vulcano island, one of the Lipari group, 6 leagues NW of Cape Bianco in Sicily.
ALUMBRES, a town of Spain, in Murcia, on the sea-coast, 3 m. SE of Carthagena.
ALUMCHUN, a town of India, in the prov. and 22 m. NW of Allahabad.
ALUM CREEK, a river of Ohio, U. S., flowing into the Big Walnut river, in Franklin co., after a course of 35 m.

ALUM HILL, a hill on the r. bank of the Peel river, in Arctic America, in about 65° 50' N lat. The soil of the surrounding district is strongly impregnated with alum, of which pure crystals were picked up on the sides of this hill by Mr. Isbester in 1845.
ALUMIERA, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. of Sienna, on the r. bank of the Brunna, 12 m. NNW of Grosetto.
ALUMPUR, a town of India, in the prov. of Allahabad, 30 m. NE of Ditteah.

ALUPKA, a village of Russia, on the south coast of the Crimea, midway between Kutkucoy and Yursova, on a stream called the Stauris Usehen. It is picturesquely situated amidst groves of fruit-trees, and gardens, in which the olive and the pomegranate attain remarkable luxuriance. [Webster.] The scenery in this district of the coast of the Crimea is so remarkably beautiful that Clarke affirms it "will admit of no comparison with any other maritime district. Such fertility and rural beauty," he adds, "are perhaps nowhere else situate equally near to the waters of any sea, nor so surrounded by grand objects. The descent towards the shore is so steep and rapid that it seems as if the villages, with their groves and gardens, might be swept by heavy rains into the deep: at the same time cliffs hanging over them menace fearful ruin by the fall of rocks which every now and then break loose." [Travels, vol. ii., 4th 8vo. edn., p. 250.] Upon and amongst prodigious fragments of these fallen rocks, the v. and its gardens are situated.
ALURED (CAPE), a point of land on the N side of Lake Ontario, 36 m. W of Newcastle; in N lat. 43° 55′, W long. 78° 25′.

ALUSHTA, a town of Russia, on the coast of the Crimea, between the mouths of the Temirdshi and the Mesarlik, on an isolated height, 20 m. E of Acmetchet. It formerly had a considerable population, and was the seat of an episcopate. It is built on a series of terraces, "so that the flat roof of one house serves as a promenade to the people of the upper one." [Webster.] The Chatyrdag is in the vicinity of this town. "We began its ascent," says Webster, "an hour after leaving A., and continued ascending an hour and a half, yet were still <math>\frac{3}{4}</math> of an hour from the top, although the height is only 1,200 ft."
ALUTA. See ALAUTA.

ALVA, a parish and town, civilly in the shire of Stirling, but locally isolated by Clackmannanshire which encircles it on the E, S, and W; while on the N it has Perthshire. It is a small district of about 4 m. in length from N to S, with an average breadth of 2 m., stretching between the Devon river and the Ochills. Pop. in 1801, 787; in 1841, 2,216, of whom 2,092 formed the pop. of the town of A., which is 7 m. NE of Stirling. Bencloch, the highest summit in the Alva range of the Ochills, has an alt. of 2,420 ft. above the level of the Devon.
ALVA (CASTREDO DE), a fortified village of Portugal, in Tras-os-Montes, on the r. bank of the Douro, 10 m. ESE of Torre de Moncorvo.—See also Alba.
ALVAH, a parish of Banffshire, on the Deveron river, 24 m. S of Banff. Pop. 1,407. ALV
ALVALLADA, a town of Portugal, in Alemtejo, near the confluence of the Campilhos, the Cerona, and the Caldao, 14 m. SW of Ferreira.
ALVAN, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik and 15 m. E of Erzerum.

ALVAR, a principality in Upper Hindostan, situated to the W of the river Jumna, between the 27th and 28th parallels, and in its greater extent, in the NW region of the prov. of Agra. It comprises, with its various adjuncts, a superficial area of about 3,000 sq. m.; and is divided into two sections,—Alvar proper or Mewat, and the Macherry territory. The former, which occupies the northern portion of the principality, is generally hilly, abounding in fastnesses and defiles, and covered to a great extent with jungly wood. The latter lies to the south, is skirted on its southern confines by a range of mountains, and presents a considerable amount of cultivation. The principal towns are Alvar, Alur or Alwur, Macherry, Rajhur, Alinuggur or Ghosauly, and Tejarah the ancient capital of Mewat. The principality and its rajah take the name by which they are usually distinguished from the town of Macherry: although Alvar is properly the capital. The largest river by which the country is intersected is the Sabee, which rises near its western confines, and runs in a NNE direction. The Laswary has its source in the Macherry country, 4 m. W of Niranpur, flows through Acberpur Ghaut to the town of Laswary, and terminates in a small lake to the W of Deeg. The inhabitants of the Mewaty district are represented as peculiarly fierce in their dispositions, and inveterately addicted to marauding expeditions. Since 1807, however, much has been effected by the conciliatory measures which have been adopted by the British legislature, and comparatively few outrages are now committed by the Mewaties. In 1803 a treaty of alliance with the Macherry rajah was entered into by Lord Lake in behalf of the British government; and two years afterwards, in consideration of this faithful adherence throughout the hazardous campaign of 1804 to the terms of that treaty, the rajah was rewarded with a large accession to his territory from that forfeited by the treachery of Pertaub Singh.—The town of Alvar, the cap. of the principality, is situated in N lat. 27° 44′, and E long. 76° 32'; 75 m. SSW of Delhi; at the base of a steep hill which rises to the height of 1,200 ft.; commanding the adjacent country, and surmounted by a fortress. To the S of the town there is a small shallow jeil. The Macherry rajah generally resides here.

ALVARADO, a sea-port of Mexico, on a river of the same name, 30 m. SSE of Vera Cruz. Its harbour is formed by the mouth of the A. river, which is completely land-locked and sheltered from every wind. The bar will admit the entrance of vessels not exceeding 10 ft. draught.—The A. river rises on the W side of the prov. of Vera Cruz, and flows through Lake Tlascotalpa.
ALVARENS, or Cahissara, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Solimoens, on a lake 15 m. from Teffe, in S lat. 3° 11'.
ALVARES, a town of Portugal, in Beira, 18 m. SSE of Arganil. Pop. 1,500.—Also a town in the island of Cuba, 65 m. NW of La Trinité.
ALVARO RODRIGUEZ, an islet off the coast of Flores in the Azores group, between Point Santa Cruz and Point Ruiva.
ALVAS, a town of Spain, in Estremadura, near the r. bank of the Alagor, near its junction with the Tagus, 8 m. N of Alcantara.
ALVASTRA, a village of Sweden, on the Wetter lake, 30 m. from Linköping. There are the remains of an ancient Cistercian abbey here.
ALVAYAZERE, a town of Portugal, in Estremadura, 15 m. N of Thomar.
ALVECHURCH, a parish in Worcestershire, 41 m. ENE of Bromsgrove. Area 6,820 acres. Pop. 1,633. The v. of A. was a borough in early times.
ALVEDISTON, or ALVESDISTON, a parish in Wiltshire, 7 m. SE of Hindon. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 263.
ALVELEY, a parish of Shropshire, on the E bank of the Severn, 6 m. SE of Bridgenorth. Area 7,640 acres. Pop. 1,062.
ALVELLOS, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Solimoens, 12 m. above the embouchure of the Coary. Cocoa and sarsaparilla are extensively cultivated in the environs.
ALVENAU, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of the Grisons, in the valley of Davos, 11 m. S by E of Coire. Pop. 400. There are sulphuretted mineral springs here.
ALVEND, a mountain-chain in Luristan, to the N of Khorremabad, whose highest summits rise above the line of perpetual snow.
ALVENSLEBEN, a town of Prussian Saxony, 14 m. NW of Magdeburg. Pop. 685.
ALVERCA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, on the r. bank of the Tagus, 15 m. NNE of Lisbon. Pop. 3,000.
ALVERDISCOTT, or ALSCOTT, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m SE of Bideford. Area 1,390 acres. Pop. 332.
ALVERDISSEN, a town of Lippe-Detmold, on the Exter, an affluent of the Weser, 12 m. NE of Detmold. Pop. 700.
ALVE'RE (SAINT), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, arrond. of Bergerac. The cant. comprises 8 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 6,801. Pop. of the com. 1,807.
ALVERINGHEM, a commune and village of Belgium, in E Flanders, 4 m. SE of Furnes. Pop. 2,753.
ALVERNIA, a hamlet of Tuscany, in the com. and 2 m. N of Chiusi-del-Casentino, on a mountain of the same name. A celebrated monastery was founded here in 1218 by Francis d'Assize.
ALVERSTOKE, a parish of Hampshire, in which Gosport is situated. Area 4,010 acres. Pop. in 1841, 13,510, of whom 8,862 were in the chapelry of Gosport,
ALVES, a parish in the shire of Elgin, on the S coast of the Moray frith, 41 m. W of Elgin. Pop. 913.
ALVESCOTT, a parish of Oxfordshire, 5 m. S by E of Burford. Area 2,690 acres. Pop. 357.
ALVESTON, a parish of Gloucestershire, 9 m. N by E of Bristol. Area 2,470 acres. Pop. 841.— Also a p. of Warwickshire, 2½ m. NE of Stratfordupon-Avon. Area 4,300 acres. Pop. 793,
ALVETON, or ALTON, a parish of Staffordshire. Area 7,470 acres. Pop. 2,390.—The township of A., on the Churnet, a branch of the Dove, is 4 m. E by S from Cheadle. Pop. 1,168.
ALVIAVARA, a village of Norway, near the right bank of the Jes river, in N lat. 69° 25', E long. 24° 40'.

ALVIE, a large parish of Inverness-shire, in Badenoch, stretching along the N bank of the Spey, and having an area of about 90 sq. m. Pop. in 1801, 1,058; in 1841, 972. Houses in 1841, 221. The Grampian chain has some of its highest summits in this parish.—The v. of A., on the margin of Loch Alvie, is 13 m. NE of Pitmain.
ALVINCZ, a town of Transylvania, in the co. of Lower Weissemburg, on the l. bank of the Marosch, opposite Boberek. Pop. 3,500.
ALVIGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, canton of Gramat. Pop. 832.
ALVINGHAM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. NE of Lowth. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 313.

ALVITO, a town of Portugal, in Alemtejo, 18 m. N of Bija, on a stream of the same name, which, rising on the E side of Monte de Viama, near Oriolas, flows into the Jarrama, 4 m. N of Odivelas. Pop. bara, in N lat. 32° 15', E long. 130° 25'. I. AMA 2,050.—Also a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavora, 8 m. SE of Sora. Pop. 3,500.
ALVOR, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Algarve, on the coast, 6 m. E of Lagos. Pop. 1,500. It has some trade in salt.
ALWALTON, a parish in Huntingdonshire, on the Nen river, 41 m. SW of Peterborough. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. 329.
ALWEIL. See HALWYL.
ALWEN, a stream of North Wales, in Denbighshire, which flows by a SE course into the Dee near Llangar.
ALWINGTON, a parish in Devonshire, 41 m. SW of Bideford. Area 5,330 acres. Pop. 392.
ALWUR. See ALVAR.
ALYRE (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Ambert. Pop. 1,074.
ALYRE-ES-MONTAGNES (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Issoire. Pop. 711.

ALYTH, a parish and town, partly in Forfarshire, partly in Perthshire, on the N side of Strathmore, intersected by the Isla river. Pop. 2,910 in 1841, of whom 190 were in Forfarshire. The King's Seat, in this p., has an alt. of 1,179 ft.; but is exceeded by Mount Blair.—The town of A. is 15 m. N of Dundee. A considerable amount of hand-weaving is performed in the town and vicinity.

ALZA, or ALYA, a river of Bavaria, in the Iser circle, which issues from the Chremsee in Upper Bavaria, and after receiving the tributary waters of the Traun, and passing Altenmarkt and Trostburg, falls into the Inn, 4 m. below Neu-Ortingen.
ALZANO MAGGIORE, a town of Lombardy, in the Milanese, 4 m. NE of Bergamo. Pop. 1,900. It has some manufactures of silk and paper.
ALZEN, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ariege, arrond. of Foix. Pop. 934.
ALZENAU, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Lower Maine, on the Kahlbach, 8 m. NNW of Aschaffenburg. Pop. 900.

ALZETTE, or ALSETTE, a river of Luxemburg, rising 6 m. E of Longwy, and flowing into the Sure at Ettelbrück, after passing Esch, Luxemburg, and Mersch, in its course of about 40 m. Its principal affluent is the Attert. By the Dutch it is called ELZE; by the Germans ELZ or ALZIG.
ALZIRA. See ALCIRA.

ALZON, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Gard. The cant. comprises 6 com., and had a pop. of 4,377 in 1831. The pop. of the com. and v., which is on the Vis, 8 m. W by S of Le Vigin, was 1,075 in 1841.

ALZONNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aude, arrond. of Carcassonne. —The cant., which comprises 11 com., had a pop. of 8,165 in 1831.—The t. is on the Fresquel, 8 m. W by N of Carcassonne. Pop. 1,629. AM, a fortress of Afghanistan, on the r. bank of the Indus, between that river and the Mabeen hills, 50 m. NE of Attock.—Also a town of Persia, in Mazanderan, 25 m. S of Farabad.
AMABA, a town of Japan, in Nifon, 35 leagues ENE of Miako.

AMACURA, or AMACORE, a river of Guayana, which, descending from the Sierra Morena d'Imataca, and running NE, after being joined by the Curriyabo and the Yarikita, flows into the estuary of the Orinoco at Navios, in S lat. 7° 30'. It appears to be navigable for steamers and small vessels to the Yarikita; but its breadth seldom exceeds 300 yards.
AMACUSA, an island of Japan, with a town of the same name, at the entrance of the gulf of Sima-
